title: Sociological Theories of the Family
examTip: In extended writing questions, always compare at least two perspectives. The strongest answers evaluate: 'While Functionalists argue X, Feminists challenge this by arguing Y, because…'
introTips: Learn one key concept and one key sociologist per perspective — that's the minimum for full marks.
Know the difference between Liberal, Radical, and Marxist feminism — they have different solutions even though they agree the family is a problem.
The New Right is not the same as Functionalism — be clear on the difference.
intro: Every time you read a headline about 'broken families' or hear a politician talk about 'family values', you're seeing sociological theory in action — whether the speaker knows it or not. How you think about the family shapes what you think causes social problems and what you think the solution is. This lesson maps out the five main theoretical frameworks sociologists use to analyse the family — and they disagree with each other profoundly.

keyPoints.heading: Functionalist perspective
keyPoints.content: George Peter Murdock (1949) studied 250 societies and concluded the nuclear family is universal because it performs four essential functions that no other institution can replace: (1) Sexual — provides a regulated outlet for sexual drives, reducing social disruption; (2) Reproductive — produces the next generation of society's members; (3) Economic — provides, pools and shares economic resources; (4) Educational (socialisation) — teaches children the norms, values and culture of society. Talcott Parsons (1955) identified two irreducible functions of the modern nuclear family: primary socialisation (children learn basic norms and values — Parsons called the family a 'personality factory') and personality stabilisation (adults find emotional stability in the family after the stresses of industrial work — the 'warm bath theory'). Parsons' functional fit thesis: the nuclear family suits industrial society because it is geographically mobile and not burdened by extended kin obligations. Criticisms: ignores conflict, abuse and inequality within families; romanticises the nuclear family; treats family dysfunction as abnormal when it is widespread; ignores family diversity; based on 1950s USA norm; the 'warm bath' theory ignores domestic violence.
writeBullets: Murdock (1949): nuclear family is universal — performs sexual, reproductive, economic, educational functions
Parsons (1955): two irreducible functions — primary socialisation and personality stabilisation
Warm bath theory: family provides emotional refuge for adults after stresses of industrial work
Functional fit thesis: nuclear family suits industrial society — mobile, not burdened by kin
Criticism: ignores abuse, conflict, inequality; treats dysfunction as abnormal; based on 1950s USA
Criticism: ignores family diversity; assumes nuclear family is the only functional form
keyPoints.memTip: Murdock's 4 functions: SREE = Sexual, Reproductive, Economic, Educational. Parsons adds: SPOTS = Socialisation, Personality stability.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse Murdock's four functions with Parsons' two — they complement each other but are different claims.

keyPoints.heading: Marxist perspective
keyPoints.content: Friedrich Engels (1884) — 'The Origin of the Family, Private Property and the State' — argued the nuclear family emerged with capitalism specifically to ensure legitimate heirs could inherit private property from fathers. Before capitalism, Engels argued, there was no need for monogamy or paternity certainty. Louis Althusser (1971) — the family is an Ideological State Apparatus (ISA): it reproduces ruling-class ideology in children — teaching obedience to authority, acceptance of hierarchy, respect for private property, and consumerism — making inequality seem natural. Eli Zaretsky (1976) — 'Capitalism, the Family and Personal Life' — the family performs three vital functions for capitalism at no cost to employers: (1) reproduces the next generation of workers; (2) provides free emotional support for exhausted workers (women absorb men's work frustrations so they return to work — the family as a 'haven in a heartless world'); (3) is a key unit of consumption (families buy consumer goods). Criticisms: ignores the genuine positive emotional aspects of family life; underestimates women's agency and resistance; many families actively challenge rather than reproduce capitalist ideology.
writeBullets: Engels (1884): nuclear family emerged with capitalism to ensure legitimate inheritance of private property
Althusser (1971): family is an Ideological State Apparatus — reproduces ruling-class ideology
Family teaches obedience, hierarchy, consumerism — makes inequality seem natural
Zaretsky (1976): three functions for capitalism — reproduces workers, provides emotional support, drives consumption
'Haven in a heartless world': women absorb workers' frustrations; family props up capitalism
Criticism: ignores positive family life; underestimates resistance; overly deterministic
keyPoints.memTip: Zaretsky's three R's for capitalism: Reproduce (workers), Release (emotional stress), Revenue (consumption). All at no cost to employers.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The concept of Ideological State Apparatus (Althusser) is widely used across AQA Sociology — learn it here and it applies to the education topic too.

keyPoints.heading: Feminist perspectives
keyPoints.content: All feminist perspectives agree: the family is a key site of women's oppression. But they differ on the cause and the solution. Liberal feminists (Oakley, 1974) focus on the unequal division of domestic labour; they want reform through equal pay legislation, flexible working, affordable childcare, and changing attitudes; they believe gradual progress is happening. Radical feminists (Millett, 1970; Greer, 1970) argue the family is the root institution of patriarchy (male dominance) — it is where gender inequality is reproduced generation after generation; some radical feminists have argued women should create separate lives from men. Marxist feminists (Benston, 1969; Delphy, 1984) argue women's domestic labour directly subsidises capitalism — producing the next generation of workers for free; women also function as a 'reserve army of labour' — brought into paid work when needed (wars, economic booms) and pushed back into domestic roles when not. All feminists highlight: domestic violence (Dobash and Dobash, 1979), the dual burden (Oakley), the triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995), and unequal decision-making (Edgell, 1980). Contemporary feminism acknowledges that progress has been made while noting that structural inequalities persist — the 'unfinished revolution'.
writeBullets: Liberal feminist (Oakley): reform unequal domestic labour — equal pay, childcare, flexible working
Radical feminist (Millett, Greer): family is root of patriarchy — reproduces gender inequality
Marxist feminist (Benston, Delphy): women's domestic labour subsidises capitalism
Reserve army of labour: women pulled into work when needed, pushed back to domestic when not
All feminists: dual burden, triple shift (Duncombe and Marsden, 1995), domestic violence (Dobash and Dobash)
Contemporary feminism: progress made but structural inequality persists — 'unfinished revolution'
keyPoints.memTip: Three feminist strands: Liberal = Law reform. Radical = Root of patriarchy. Marxist = Reproduces capitalism. LRM.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA often asks students to compare feminist perspectives — being able to distinguish liberal, radical, and Marxist feminism is a higher-mark skill.

keyPoints.heading: New Right perspective
keyPoints.content: The New Right is not a unified academic theory but a political-ideological position influential in the 1980s–1990s. Key thinkers: Charles Murray (1984) — 'Losing Ground'; Patricia Morgan. Core argument: the nuclear family (married, heterosexual couple, with biological children, male breadwinner) is the natural and most functional family form, providing the best environment for children. Evidence cited: children from two-parent families achieve better educational outcomes and show lower crime rates than children from lone-parent families. Murray's 'underclass' thesis: father-absent lone-parent families (especially) create an underclass — boys without male role models turn to crime and gang culture; girls become young mothers to access welfare benefits. The welfare state is blamed for enabling and incentivising family breakdown. Criticisms: (a) victim-blaming — ignores structural causes of poverty; (b) correlation ≠ causation — poverty causes both family breakdown and poor outcomes, not the other way around; (c) idealises a family form that never existed for most working-class or ethnic minority people; (d) ignores domestic violence within nuclear families; (e) relies on sexist and homophobic assumptions about gender roles and sexuality.
writeBullets: Murray (1984): nuclear family (married, heterosexual, male breadwinner) is the natural ideal
Lone-parent families (especially father-absent) create an 'underclass' — crime, welfare dependency
Welfare state blamed for incentivising family breakdown
Criticism: victim-blaming — ignores poverty and structural causes
Criticism: correlation ≠ causation — poverty causes bad outcomes, not lone-parenthood alone
Criticism: sexist and homophobic assumptions; ignores domestic violence within nuclear families
keyPoints.memTip: New Right = nuclear family is 'natural' and best. Everything else = social problem. Murray's underclass = poverty blamed on family structure.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students should be able to criticise the New Right using the correlation ≠ causation argument — this is a strong evaluative point.

keyPoints.heading: Postmodern perspective
keyPoints.content: Judith Stacey (1990) argued there is no single 'the family' — just 'families'; diversity of family arrangements is the reality in postmodern society and should be celebrated, not pathologised. Ulrich Beck and Elisabeth Beck-Gernsheim (1995) — the 'negotiated family': relationships are now based on individual negotiation and choice rather than tradition, religion, or obligation. This is individualisation — people construct their own life narratives including family arrangements. It is liberating but also creates instability and anxiety. Anthony Giddens (1992) — the 'pure relationship': intimate relationships are now formed and maintained only as long as they provide emotional satisfaction to both partners; based on emotional democracy and personal fulfilment, not economic necessity or tradition. When they stop working, people leave — hence rising divorce. Jeffrey Weeks — 'chosen families' or 'families of choice': especially in LGBTQ+ communities, close friends take on family functions when biological family is unsupportive. Criticisms: postmodernism overstates the freedom of individual choice — many people (poor women, people in abusive relationships, those in conservative communities) have very limited choices; ignores structural constraints; ignores that the majority of children are still raised in two-parent households.
writeBullets: Stacey (1990): no single 'family' — just 'families'; celebrate diversity
Beck and Beck-Gernsheim (1995): 'negotiated family' — based on choice not tradition; individualisation
Individualisation: people construct own life narratives including family — liberating but anxious
Giddens (1992): 'pure relationship' — maintained only while emotionally fulfilling; explains rising divorce
Weeks: 'chosen families' — close friends as family especially in LGBTQ+ communities
Criticism: overstates freedom of choice; ignores structural constraints; poor and marginalised people lack choice
keyPoints.memTip: Postmodern family key words: Stacey = diversity; Beck = negotiated; Giddens = pure relationship; Weeks = chosen. SBGW.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Giddens' 'pure relationship' is the most cited postmodern concept in AQA Sociology — link it to rising divorce rates.

tables.title: Five perspectives on the family compared
rows: Functionalism
Nuclear family is universal and performs essential functions for society
Warm bath theory; primary socialisation; functional fit
Murdock (1949); Parsons (1955)
Ignores conflict, abuse and diversity; based on 1950s USA norm
Marxism
Family reproduces capitalism — workers, ideology and consumption — at no cost to employers
Ideological State Apparatus; haven in a heartless world
Engels (1884); Althusser (1971); Zaretsky (1976)
Ignores positive emotional life; underestimates resistance and agency
Feminism
Family is a key site of women's oppression — domestic labour, violence, power inequality
Dual burden; triple shift; patriarchy; reserve army
Oakley (1974); Millett (1970); Dobash and Dobash (1979)
Liberal feminism: too slow. Radical: too extreme. All: ignores progress made
New Right
Nuclear family is the natural ideal; other forms cause social problems — crime, welfare dependency
Underclass; welfare dependency; family breakdown
Murray (1984); Morgan
Victim-blaming; ignores structural causes; correlation ≠ causation; sexist and homophobic assumptions
Postmodernism
No single 'normal' family; diversity should be celebrated; individuals choose own arrangements
Pure relationship; individualisation; negotiated family; chosen families
Stacey (1990); Giddens (1992); Beck and Beck-Gernsheim (1995)
Overstates freedom of choice; ignores structural constraints; most children still in two-parent households

0.question: According to Parsons (1955), what is the 'warm bath theory'?
options: The family provides emotional stability and refuge for adults after the stresses of industrial work
Families in warm climates have stronger bonds than those in cold countries
Bathing children together is an important part of primary socialisation
The emotional warmth of the nuclear family makes it the ideal environment for children
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Parsons' warm bath theory says the family acts like a warm bath — it relaxes and restores exhausted workers, providing the emotional stability they need to keep functioning in the capitalist economy.
0.wrongFeedback: The warm bath theory (Parsons, 1955) refers to personality stabilisation — the idea that the family provides emotional refuge for adults after the stresses of industrial work, like a warm bath that soothes and restores.

2.question: What do Marxist feminists like Benston and Delphy mean when they say women are a 'reserve army of labour'?
options: Women are brought into paid work when the economy needs them, then pushed back into domestic roles when they are not needed
Women do military service in reserve units rather than front-line combat
Women's domestic work creates a reserve of unpaid value that capitalism draws on
Women are employed in low-skilled jobs that men are reluctant to take
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The reserve army of labour is women who are called into paid employment when needed (wars, economic booms) and returned to domestic roles when demand drops — this flexibility serves capitalism's interests.
2.wrongFeedback: The 'reserve army of labour' (Benston, Delphy) refers to how capitalism uses women as a flexible labour supply — drawn into paid work when needed, pushed back to domestic roles when not — a pool the economy can tap at will.

4.question: Anthony Giddens (1992) introduced the concept of the 'pure relationship'. What does this mean?
options: A relationship maintained only as long as it provides emotional fulfilment to both partners
A relationship between two people of the same social class
A relationship free from sexual activity — based purely on emotional closeness
A nuclear family where both parents are biologically related to all children
4.correctFeedback: Correct! Giddens' 'pure relationship' (1992) is formed and maintained only because it satisfies both partners. When it no longer does — people leave. This postmodern concept helps explain rising divorce rates.
4.wrongFeedback: Giddens' 'pure relationship' (1992) is a postmodern concept: relationships are now maintained only as long as they provide emotional fulfilment to both partners. When satisfaction ends, so does the relationship — which explains higher divorce rates.

commonMistakes: Confusing Functionalism and the New Right — both value the nuclear family, but Functionalists see it as naturally evolved to meet society's needs; New Right thinkers see alternatives as moral failures requiring policy intervention.
Saying 'Marxists think the family is bad for workers' without specifying why — the key point is that it reproduces capitalism at no cost to employers.
Treating feminism as one single view — Liberal, Radical, and Marxist feminism have very different arguments and solutions.
Forgetting that postmodern perspectives are generally positive about family diversity — they celebrate it, they don't just describe it.
Not evaluating perspectives — simply describing each theory without any critique will not reach the highest marks in extended questions.
examTips: For a 12-mark question on perspectives, use the structure: introduce the perspective → key argument + key concept → named sociologist → evaluate with a counter-perspective.
Always contrast the New Right and Feminist perspectives on the family — they interpret the same family forms in completely opposite ways.

To criticise Functionalism effectively: 'Functionalism ignores the dark side of family life — 1 in 4 women experience domestic violence (Dobash and Dobash, 1979) — which contradicts the warm bath theory.'
Postmodernism is great for evaluation conclusions: 'In a postmodern society characterised by individualisation (Beck, 1995), the very idea of one 'correct' family form becomes meaningless.'
revisionCardBullets: Functionalism (Murdock, 1949): nuclear family universal — sexual, reproductive, economic, educational functions
Parsons (1955): primary socialisation + personality stabilisation (warm bath theory); functional fit thesis
Marxism (Engels, 1884): nuclear family ensures inheritance of private property under capitalism
Althusser (1971): family as Ideological State Apparatus — reproduces ruling-class ideology
Zaretsky (1976): family reproduces workers, provides emotional support, drives consumption — for capitalism
Liberal feminism (Oakley): reform domestic inequality — equal pay, childcare, flexible working
Radical feminism (Millett, Greer): family is root of patriarchy — reproduces male dominance
Marxist feminism (Benston, Delphy): women's domestic labour subsidises capitalism; reserve army of labour
New Right (Murray, 1984): nuclear family is the ideal; lone-parent families create underclass
Postmodernism (Stacey, 1990): celebrate diversity; no single 'normal' family
Giddens (1992): 'pure relationship' — maintained only while emotionally fulfilling; explains rising divorce
Beck and Beck-Gernsheim (1995): individualisation — people construct own family arrangements; liberating but anxious
